usage_note: Patients are differentiated by surgical specialty, procedure complexity, urgency class, and admission type — multiple entity classes flow through the same perioperative process with different service time distributions and routing rules.
Elective vs. emergency cases and day-of-surgery vs. inpatient admissions create distinct patient classes with different priorities and routing.
Elective surgeries are scheduled days to weeks in advance with stochastic punctuality.
Elective volume peaks weekday mornings; emergency arrivals follow a Poisson rate that varies by time of day and day of week.
Emergency cases carry priority attributes that preempt scheduled elective cases.
Emergency cases bump scheduled cases from OR assignments via priority-based service discipline rather than FIFO.
Elective ORs run 7am-5pm weekdays; emergency capability is 24/7 with on-call staffing.
Surgeons have specialty certifications; anesthesiologists have specialty certifications; nurses have scrub vs. circulator roles — not every resource is interchangeable.
PACU beds are shared across all ORs and are a common bottleneck; surgical teams and anesthesia staff are also shared across competing cases.
Post-PACU destination depends on clinical state (ward, ICU, or discharge); OR contamination triggers case rescheduling.
When PACU is full, OR cases stall at the OR-PACU handoff, creating upstream backpressure that blocks the OR from accepting the next case.
Scenario 4 (Parallel Induction of Anesthesia) explicitly models anesthesia induction in an adjacent room concurrent with the current case as a parallel-path optimization.
